01a_quotes.title: No Great Equalizer: Experimental Evidence on Productivity Effects of Generative AI Use in the UK Labor Market

study_design: we conducted a large pre-registered online experiment with a sample of the UK working age population which randomly assigned participants to treatments that encouraged or discouraged the use of ChatGPT and then asked them to complete a set of realistic work tasks
For our pre-registered online experiment, we recruited 1,041 respondents who had ChatGPT accounts through the survey company YouGov.
The sample was split into a treatment group (N = 504) which was encouraged to use ChatGPT and a control group (N = 537) which was instructed to complete the survey without using ChatGPT.
Fieldwork for the survey took place from 19th – 28th July 2023, following a pre-test in early July 2023.
We estimated the local average treatment effect (LATE) of using generative AI with a two-stage least squares(2SLS)procedure.
task: Study participants performed three tasks of varying complexity (see Table 1) while being instructed to use ChatGPT (treatment group) or not use ChatGPT (control group).
In the first task, participants were presented with an email addressing a hypothetical workplace dispute and were asked to make any improvements they deemed appropriate.
In the second task, respondents evaluated the persuasiveness of two texts of about 500 words, each presenting opposing views on the merits of a universal basic income.
In the final task we asked participants to answer three short questions about a complex text on the economic history of Ireland.
We evaluated productivity based on two measures: time to complete the task and answer quality according to pre-specified grading criteria.
population: we recruited 1,041 respondents who had ChatGPT accounts through the survey company YouGov
our sample is larger and approximates the demographic composition of the UK working-age population
YouGov provided demographic information, including age, sex, education, occupation, income, and other variables
Despite their best efforts, YouGov could not at the time provide a fully representative sample of ChatGPT users. For example, men are overrepresented in the sample (see Table S7).
The median time to complete the survey was approximately 28 minutes.
arms: The sample was split into a treatment group (N = 504) which was encouraged to use ChatGPT and a control group (N = 537) which was instructed to complete the survey without using ChatGPT.
outcome_definitions: We obtained two measures of respondents' performance on the tasks. First, we evaluated their answers on a five-point scale using GPT-4 and a detailed grading scheme
As a second measure of productivity, we recorded the time (in seconds) respondents spent on each of the tasks.
we top-coded the time measure for each task at 900 seconds to avoid obtaining results that are driven by a small number of respondents who likely became distracted
statistical_results: The difference in time spent and grade achieved between treatment and control group is highly statistically significant (p< 0.001) and sizeable for all three tasks.
Predicted time savings range between 101 and 235 seconds (0.54 to 1.04 standard deviations (SD)), while performance increases amount to between 0.65 to 2.14 points (0.61 to 1.45 SD).
respondents in the control group achieved the highest average scores in the email task (3.1), with successively lower grades in the assessment task (2.4) and the comprehension task (2.0)
AI use increased the average score in the — arguably least complicated — email task by 1.26 points (0.92 SD), but boosted scores in the assessment task by only 0.65 points (0.61 SD).
the return to AI use was greatest in the comprehension task with 2.14 points (1.45 SD)

Compared to the control group, within-task variation in grades in the treatment group is lower but only marginally statistically significantly different for the email task (p< 0.1), significantly higher for the assessment task (p< 0.001), and significantly lower for the comprehension task (p< 0.05).
Men, respondents under 40, degree holders, and managers and professionals are between 4 and 9 percentage points more likely to report using ChatGPT at least once per week.
Despite the greater initial familiarity of men with ChatGPT and the well-established "gender digital divide" in favor of men (OECD, 2018), we find remarkably similar treatment effects
Both younger and older workers become more productive with generative AI in equal measure.
we find no evidence to suggest that more educated workers or those working in professional or managerial occupations might monopolize the benefits of generative AI
in the assessment task, the LATE for frequent users is more than double that for infrequent users
In the comprehension task, the treatment effect is 30 percent larger for frequent users, erasing the performance differential observed in the control group.
Average inter-coder agreement between ChatGPT and human coders, as measured by intra-class correlations, was similar to human- human inter-coder agreement (see Table S9)
ambiguity_or_caveats: It was necessary to sample from ChatGPT users since at the time it was not possible to embed ChatGPT directly in the survey for technical and privacy reasons.
This means that we can directly observe only treatment assignment, not compliance.
the focus on text-based tasks entails that we ignore other use cases where generative AI has proved useful, such as coding tasks or creative work
Due to sample size limitations, we could not investigate whether AI training affects people's competence in using AI
Furthermore, due to survey time constraints, we did not collect a baseline measure of task-specific skills which would have allowed for within-individual comparisons
The same reason also prevented us from including an even wider range of tasks which would have enhanced the generalizability of our findings.
Our use of monetary incentives to increase effort may have had a stronger effect on men

Most importantly, it was not possible at the time to administer the survey to a fully representative sample of the UK working-age population — instead, our sample was limited to individuals in YouGov's panel who already had a ChatGPT account.
Our participants are therefore likely to be more open to new technologies than the population at large.
our study illustrates the effects of generative AI capabilities at a particular moment in time; we acknowledge that future iterations may yield different effects

However, it is important to bear in mind that our sample likely includes comparatively tech-savvy older workers and our results therefore potentially understate the advantage to younger workers in a fully representative sample.
This expectation, however, was not confirmed.
we find no consistent pattern with regard to within-task variation inperformance

01a_quotes.notes: This is a pre-registered randomized controlled trial (RCT) examining the productivity effects of ChatGPT use across three text-based tasks of varying complexity. The study challenges the emerging narrative that generative AI is an inherently equalizing force. Key finding: while ChatGPT improved productivity for all demographic groups similarly, the benefits were not uniformly distributed across task complexity as hypothesized. The paper emphasizes that task ambiguity (in addition to complexity) matters for AI usefulness, and that frequent AI users benefit more in ambiguous tasks. The study includes extensive supplementary materials with power analyses, Bayes factors, and robustness checks.

01_extraction.title: No Great Equalizer: Experimental Evidence on Productivity Effects of Generative AI Use in the UK Labor Market
01_extraction.study_design_description: A large pre-registered online experiment with 1,041 UK working-age ChatGPT users recruited through YouGov. Participants were randomly assigned to a treatment group (N = 504) encouraged to use ChatGPT or a control group (N = 537) instructed to complete tasks without ChatGPT. The local average treatment effect (LATE) was estimated using two-stage least squares (2SLS). Fieldwork took place from 19–28 July 2023, following a pre-test in early July 2023.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Study participants performed three tasks of varying complexity: (1) an email task addressing a hypothetical workplace dispute, (2) an assessment task evaluating the persuasiveness of two opposing texts on universal basic income, and (3) a comprehension task answering three short questions about a complex text on Irish economic history. Productivity was evaluated based on two measures: time to complete the task (in seconds, top-coded at 900 seconds per task) and answer quality according to pre-specified grading criteria using a five-point scale.
01_extraction.population_description: 1,041 respondents who had ChatGPT accounts, recruited through the survey company YouGov. The sample approximates the demographic composition of the UK working-age population and includes demographic information on age, sex, education, occupation, income, and other variables. However, the sample is not fully representative of ChatGPT users; for example, men are overrepresented. Participants are likely more open to new technologies than the population at large. The median time to complete the survey was approximately 28 minutes.

01_extraction.notes: This is a pre-registered RCT examining ChatGPT productivity effects. The study uses 2SLS to estimate LATE, with treatment assignment as the instrument. Regression coefficients are reported with standard errors in parentheses and significance stars (***). The paper reports control group means for task scores (email: 3.1, assessment: 2.4, comprehension: 2.0) but not standard deviations. Time savings and performance increases are reported both in raw units and standardized (SD) units. The sample is limited to ChatGPT users and is not fully representative of the UK working-age population; men are overrepresented. The study did not collect baseline measures of task-specific skills. Heterogeneous treatment effects by frequency of AI use are mentioned qualitatively (frequent users benefit more in assessment and comprehension tasks) but specific coefficients are not provided in the quotes. Inter-coder agreement between ChatGPT and human coders was similar to human-human agreement.

02_method.rationale: The study describes random assignment: 'Participants were randomly assigned to a treatment group (N = 504) encouraged to use ChatGPT or a control group (N = 537) instructed to complete tasks without ChatGPT.' This is an online experiment conducted through YouGov, not in a lab or real work setting, making it a straightforward RCT.

per_outcome.operationalization: Time in seconds from task start to completion, measured by experimental platform, top-coded at 900 seconds
Time in seconds from task start to completion, measured by experimental platform, top-coded at 900 seconds
Time in seconds from task start to completion, measured by experimental platform, top-coded at 900 seconds
Five-point rubric score evaluating answer quality, graded by GPT-4 using a detailed grading scheme
Five-point rubric score evaluating answer quality, graded by GPT-4 using a detailed grading scheme
Five-point rubric score evaluating answer quality, graded by GPT-4 using a detailed grading scheme

04_onet.rationale: The participants performed three distinct information-processing tasks: composing written responses to workplace scenarios, evaluating and comparing persuasive texts, and comprehending/answering questions about complex written material. These activities—writing emails, assessing arguments, and extracting information from texts—are all core examples of 'Processing Information,' which O*NET defines as compiling, coding, categorizing, calculating, tabulating, auditing, or verifying information or data. While the tasks involve some communication elements, the substantive work being measured is the cognitive processing of information across multiple formats and complexity levels, not the performance of a complete occupational role.

alternates.onet_label: Communicating with Supervisors, Peers, or Subordinates

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Single AI treatment arm where participants were encouraged to use ChatGPT. This is a between-subjects design with random assignment to treatment (encouraged AI use) vs control (discouraged AI use).
llm_extracted.notes: Table S1 Panel A provides the baseline constant (314.465 seconds, SE=7.554) which represents the control group mean for email task completion time. The regression coefficient (-134.010, SE=14.548) represents the treatment effect. With both the baseline mean and the regression coefficient in levels, we can compute log(1 + coef/baseline) = log(1 + (-134.010/314.465)) = log(0.574). The outcome is time in seconds (top-coded at 900), so smaller is better. The paper reports this as a 2SLS/LATE estimate where treatment assignment instruments for actual AI use.
source_quotes: Used AI −134.010*** (14.548) Constant 314.465*** (7.554) for Email Time in Table S1 Panel A: Second stage
We estimated the local average treatment effect (LATE) of using generative AI with a two-stage least squares (2SLS) procedure
Time (in seconds) respondents spent on each of the tasks. Since some respondents spent a large amount of time on a single task, we top-coded the time measure for each task at 900 seconds
Predicted time savings range between 101 and 235 seconds (0.54 to 1.04 standard deviations (SD))

computed.notes: Table S1 Panel A provides the baseline constant (314.465 seconds, SE=7.554) which represents the control group mean for email task completion time. The regression coefficient (-134.010, SE=14.548) represents the treatment effect. With both the baseline mean and the regression coefficient in levels, we can compute log(1 + coef/baseline) = log(1 + (-134.010/314.465)) = log(0.574). The outcome is time in seconds (top-coded at 900), so smaller is better. The paper reports this as a 2SLS/LATE estimate where treatment assignment instruments for actual AI use.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI arm available: chatgpt_encouraged. Control arm is the human-only baseline.
llm_extracted.notes: Mean for AI arm calculated as 3.1 + 1.26 = 4.36 from reported effect. Standardized effect explicitly reported as '0.92 SD' which we treat as Cohen's d. P-value reported as inequality 'p < 0.001' so exact value set to null. Regression coefficient confirms the 1.262 point increase with p < 0.001.
source_quotes: AI use increased the average score in the — arguably least complicated — email task by 1.26 points (0.92 SD)

computed.notes: Mean for AI arm calculated as 3.1 + 1.26 = 4.36 from reported effect. Standardized effect explicitly reported as '0.92 SD' which we treat as Cohen's d. P-value reported as inequality 'p < 0.001' so exact value set to null. Regression coefficient confirms the 1.262 point increase with p < 0.001.
